name: Polygonal Masonry
description: Polygonal masonry refers to a style of construction employing irregular, multi-sided stones that are fitted together tightly without mortar. This technique is found in various ancient sites across the globe, most notably in the Mediterranean region, including Peru, and Japan. Proponents of alternative theories often suggest that the precision and complexity of this construction indicate advanced, possibly lost, technologies or the involvement of pre-historic advanced civilizations, extraterrestrial entities, or unknown ancient cultures predating known historical records. They argue that the seemingly random shapes and tight fits are beyond the capabilities of primitive or even early historical societies. The absence of mortar is also cited as evidence of superior craftsmanship. Some theories propose that polygonal masonry possesses unique energetic properties or was used for specific purposes beyond basic construction. Mainstream archaeology attributes polygonal masonry to various cultures and time periods, explaining its presence in different regions as independent developments. While acknowledging the skill involved, archaeologists argue that the construction was achieved through careful planning, precise stone cutting using tools available at the time (bronze, iron, stone hammers), and a 'trial and error' approach. The variation in quality and precision across different sites and even within single structures supports the idea of evolving techniques rather than a single, advanced source. The lack of mortar is explained by the precise fitting of stones, which creates a stable structure through interlocking and weight distribution. Claims of advanced technology are generally dismissed due to the absence of further corroborating evidence and the demonstrable feasibility of traditional construction methods.
subtitle: Ancient stonework with interlocking polygonal blocks.

claims.claim: Polygonal masonry requires advanced, lost technologies.
claims.evidence: Irregular shapes and tight fits appear too complex for primitive tools.
claims.criticism: Achievable with available tools through careful planning, precise cutting, and trial-and-error; variations in quality suggest evolving techniques.

claims.claim: Polygonal masonry is evidence of pre-historic advanced civilizations.
claims.evidence: Presence across geographically distant locations suggests a common, advanced origin.
claims.criticism: Independent development in different cultures and time periods is more plausible, given the specific characteristics of each site.

claims.claim: Polygonal masonry has unique energetic properties.
claims.evidence: Some claim to detect energy fields or unusual magnetic anomalies near these structures.
claims.criticism: No scientifically verifiable evidence supports these claims; anecdotal evidence and subjective experiences are not reliable.

keyFigures.name: Graham Hancock
keyFigures.role: Promotes the idea of lost advanced civilizations responsible for ancient megalithic structures.

keyFigures.name: Christopher Dunn
keyFigures.role: Argues for advanced machining techniques in ancient Egyptian construction, often linked to similar theories about polygonal masonry.

keyFigures.name: Brian Fagan
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist specializing in ancient technology and construction, offering mainstream explanations for ancient building techniques.

mythologyConnections: Legends of gods teaching construction techniques

timeline.event: Development of polygonal masonry in Mycenaean Greece and other Mediterranean regions.
Inca civilization utilizes polygonal masonry in Peru.
Alternative theories emerge, linking polygonal masonry to lost civilizations and advanced technologies.

sources.author: David Hatcher Childress
sources.work: Technology of the Gods: The Incredible Sciences of the Ancients
sources.title: David Hatcher Childress

variants: Ashlar masonry (when used in similar alternative theories)
